en: Albert, the forest is invading.
I'm not quite understanding this.
To celebrate
Christmas, we put a tree in the house.
ko: 크리스마스를 기념해서 집 안에 나무를 놓는 거예요.

en: They do this in Germany, do they?
Hmm, well
that explains a lot.
Perhaps you've confused the English for tree with the
English for grandfather clock.
They're both made of wood.
And how exactly does
the tree relate to the baby Jesus?
They did have trees in Bethlehem.
Albert, I
know you've had a tremendously hard year, a lot of projects you've worked very hard on.
ko: 당신이 올해 정말 힘들었다는 거 알아, 열심히 한 프로젝트들이 많이

en: falling through, but has it really come to this?
ko: 무산됐지만, 정말 이 지경까지 온 거야?

en: Yes, dear, you might think it
symbolizes eternal life.
ko: 영원한 생명을 상징한다고 생각할 수도 있겠지.

en: I think it symbolizes squirrels getting in the
house.
Careful, children, I don't want you to get pine cholera.
ko: 조심해, 얘들아, 솔잎 콜레라 걸리면 안 돼.

en: Well, I suppose it can
stay, but I'll need a footman to check the branches for urchins.
ko: 있겠지만, 하인이 가지 사이에 부랑아가 없는지 확인해야 해.

en: Nobody gets in
here for free. Let me get this straight.
We put candles, lighted candles, in a tree
ko: 우리가 촛불을, 불 붙은 촛불을, 나무에 꽂고

en: in the house.
Does anyone else see where I'm coming from here?
Are you sure it's
not a practical joke that Gladstone's playing on us?
Because he does play a lot
of pranks and absolutely none of them are funny.
And now we're sticking little
ornaments on it. It gets safer and safer.
I have to say, I always knew German
culture was crazy, Albert.
I mean, have you met my mother?
First pretzels and now
this?
All right, Albert, you can keep the tree, but next year I'm going to do
ko: 좋아, Albert, 나무는 놔둬도 돼. 하지만 내년에는 내가

en: something crazy for Christmas, like putting a horse in the dining room.
ko: 뭔가 미친 짓을 할 거야 크리스마스에, 식당에 말을 들여놓는다든지.

en: And now what do we do? Do we touch it?
We just look at it?
You know, there's like a
hundred of these outside we could look at.
ko: 이런 거 백 개는 있어서 구경할 수 있는데.

en: Oh, Albert, you didn't tell me it
smelled so good.
We need ten more.
